title: Jacket potato with tuna mayo and sweetcorn
subtitle: A crisp-skinned baked potato with a cold tuna and sweetcorn filling
excerpt: Baked potato with tuna mayo and sweetcorn is the lunchtime stalwart of every British sandwich bar and school canteen. The contrast of the hot, crisp skin with the cold, creamy filling is the whole point, and it is even better made at home.
sourceNotes: British lunch tradition. The tuna-mayo jacket potato became widespread in British catering during the 1970s and is now one of the most popular lunchtime options across the country.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The tuna filling can be made a day ahead and kept chilled. Bake the potatoes fresh.

content.text: Jacket potato with tuna and sweetcorn combines a properly baked, crisp-skinned potato with a cold, creamy filling. The method for the potato is the same as any good jacket: oil, salt, high heat, no foil. The filling takes minutes to put together while the potato bakes.

items.prepNote: large floury variety, about 300 g each

content.text: Heat the oven to 220°C fan. Scrub and dry the potatoes. Prick each one all over with a fork, rub with oil, and coat with sea salt flakes. Place directly on the oven rack and bake for 70 to 75 minutes until the skin is crisp and the flesh gives easily to a knife.
Flake the tuna into a bowl. Add the sweetcorn, mayonnaise, and lemon juice. Mix gently and season with black pepper. Taste and add salt if needed (the tuna often provides enough).
Cut a cross into the top of each hot potato, squeeze the ends to open, and spoon the tuna filling generously on top.

items.cause: Too much mayonnaise, or the tuna or sweetcorn was not drained properly.
items.fix: Drain both well. Start with 2 tablespoons of mayonnaise and add more if needed.

items.symptom: The potato skin is chewy rather than crisp
items.cause: Foil was used, or the temperature was too low.
items.fix: Never wrap in foil. High heat and a dry surface are what make the skin crisp.

items.symptom: The potato takes much longer than expected
items.cause: The potato was very large, or the oven runs too hot or too cold.
items.fix: Test with a knife through the thickest part. There should be no resistance.

content.text: The baked potato with tuna filling became a fixture of British high-street lunch during the 1970s and 1980s, when the jacket potato bar was a feature of most market towns. It travels well as a school or work lunch, holds for hours, and requires nothing but a microwave to reheat, though the oven gives a far better skin.
